manuscript.title: Criminality V2
manuscript.target_audience: Readers of literary fiction interested in queer family-making, systemic critiques of surveillance and criminalization, and morally ambiguous crime narratives, similar to audiences for writers who blend memoir-like intimacy with political and philosophical inquiry.

evidence.snippet: I needed to feel his energy and was desperate to know if he was there waiting for me.
Irmak raised the subject of having Utku’s children.

criteria.rationale: The pairing of the unnamed narrator’s queer, grief-driven parenthood with MJ’s risky journey toward Desire in the Coachella Valley yields a layered, high-concept frame that feels both intimate and political. Scenes with Utku and Irmak in the Istanbul highlands and with MJ under the gaze of Five Eyes and the L3 Communications millimeter wave machine all support a cohesive argument about criminality, love, and surveillance that Aralık can interrogate. The pressure comes from the father’s decision to tell Aralık a morally ambiguous story about MJ at bedtime, which risks unsettling a child but promises a more truthful understanding of systems that punish people like MJ and Utku; because MJ’s eventual meeting with Desire is deferred, some of that conceptual pressure is intentionally left unresolved for later payoff.

evidence.snippet: He would take the chance though.
I took Irmak’s tiny hand into mine, studied her smooth olive-colored skin...
I decided to introduce him to a character named MJ who had dipped his toes into the sometimes icy cold...

criteria.rationale: Narrative momentum arises from the unnamed narrator’s bedtime ritual with Aralık and from MJ’s stepwise movement through the United Empire airport, past cameras and the L3 Communications millimeter wave machine, under the unseen gaze of Five Eyes. MJ’s refusal to enter the scanner and his fixation on the young fawn keep pressure alive, but prolonged exposition about statutes and intelligence alliances pauses forward motion relative to the emotional engine centered on Utku and Irmak. When the father ends the MJ story with “That’s the end of the story” just as MJ goes to the airplane washroom, the consequence of this accumulated pressure is intentionally deferred, which fits the reflective mode but softens immediate propulsion.

recommendations.action: Increase the tension in scenes by introducing stakes that directly affect the protagonist’s goals.
recommendations.symptom: Stakes or decision pressure diffuses before reaching the reader, weakening narrative urgency.
recommendations.mechanism: the stakes signal arrives too late in the passage, diffusing narrative urgency at the turn
recommendations.specific_fix: insert one concrete stakes beat that lands the deferred decision at the current scene turn
recommendations.reader_effect: This will create a more compelling narrative momentum.
recommendations.anchor_snippet: I needed to feel his energy and was desperate to know if he was there waiting for me.
recommendations.expected_impact: This will create a more compelling narrative momentum.
recommendations.candidate_text_a: I needed to feel his energy, fearing that without it, my connection to Utku would fade forever.
recommendations.candidate_text_b: I was desperate to know if he was there waiting for me, knowing that my grief could consume me if I failed.
recommendations.candidate_text_c: I needed to feel his energy, or I risked losing the last thread connecting me to Utku.

evidence.snippet: I’d wish I had. But then there were many things I wished for where it concerned Utku.
Irmak had a special bond with him.
I still live in Toronto and have been retired from Pratt & Whitney Canada for fourteen years...

criteria.rationale: The unnamed narrator, shaped by Utku’s death and the sacred highland meeting place near Istanbul, comes across as psychologically coherent in his choice to both protect and challenge Aralık with MJ’s morally complex story. Irmak’s pansexual history with Utku and her later proposal in the highlands to have a child with the narrator reveal layered motivations around Desire and legacy, while MJ’s mixture of fear and bravado at the airport and his fixation on the young fawn enrich him beyond a stock smuggler. Pressure gathers as each of these characters makes decisions—Irmak about conceiving in Canada, MJ about refusing the L3 Communications scan, the narrator about narrating—and those decisions’ full emotional consequences for Aralık and MJ remain partially deferred in this excerpt.

evidence.snippet: I still live in Toronto and have been retired from Pratt & Whitney Canada for fourteen years.
I thought back then if I ever were to have a son, I would name him as such.
I recall talking about this with Utku, since I thought it odd that a lesbian would want sex with a gay guy.

criteria.rationale: The first-person voice of the unnamed narrator, whether describing Utku’s absence in the Istanbul highlands or unpacking MJ’s passage through the L3 Communications machine and Five Eyes, is consistently reflective, slightly formal, and gently pedagogical toward Aralık. This register lets the same voice plausibly quote Irmak’s “Ahhhh, consider what?” and explain the Demagogue and Dark Overlords in terms Aralık can link to Darth Vader, while still carrying grief-heavy lines about myocarditis and sacred places. Pressure builds as the narrative voice must balance honesty about MJ’s body-cavity smuggling and Desire’s festival with the bedtime context; the decision to end with “You have to come to your conclusions about the ending” keeps the voice intact but knowingly leaves Aralık and the reader in thoughtful frustration.

recommendations.action: Recast one summary sentence as close-third free indirect discourse to restore psychic distance; Several lines around the passage near “I still live in Toronto…” summarize effect before dramatizing cause. Re-ordering the beat sequence because the same didactic tone occasionally dominates MJ’s more suspenseful scenes,.

evidence.snippet: The desert sun was a respite from the cold tundra where MJ lived.
I looked at them often. In the weeks following Utku’s passing, I’d spend hours walking in front of his parents’ home...
The congregation was far from civilization – allowing them to partake - without judgment - in rituals...

criteria.rationale: Key scenes—Irmak and the unnamed narrator in the Istanbul highlands, MJ approaching the L3 Communications scanner under Five Eyes, and the Toronto bedtime exchange with Aralık—are clearly staged and anchored in specific settings, but several of them lean heavily on narrated explanation rather than in-scene beats. Pressure accumulates as MJ progresses from check-in through cameras and metal detectors toward the millimeter wave machine, yet the tension is occasionally undercut by stretches of legal commentary about Schedule 1 and 2 drugs and United Empire thresholds. The consequences of these scenes’ central moves, such as Irmak’s proposal or MJ’s choice to refuse the scan and later go to the airplane washroom, are mostly deferred to off-page time.

recommendations.action: Incorporate more sensory details to enrich the atmosphere of key scenes.
recommendations.symptom: Scene cause-and-effect sequencing is disrupted, reducing structural coherence.
recommendations.mechanism: the causal sequencing is inverted, so the consequence lands before the trigger, losing scene-turn coherence
recommendations.specific_fix: split one long descriptive passage and move one image after the causal action beat
recommendations.reader_effect: This will deepen reader immersion and emotional engagement.

recommendations.expected_impact: This will deepen reader immersion and emotional engagement.

evidence.snippet: “What is an anthropomorphic deity?”
Ahhhh, consider what?”
“So MJ was left-winged!” Aralık stated excitedly.

criteria.rationale: Dialogue between Irmak and the unnamed narrator in the Istanbul highlands, and between the narrator and Aralık in the Toronto bedroom, feels natural in cadence and clearly conveys relationship dynamics and teaching moments. Irmak’s tentative “Ahhhh, consider what?” and direct “Having a child with me” line reveal both her vulnerability and boldness, while Aralık’s “Can I stay up later if I get the next one!?” shows playful negotiation. The pressure in these exchanges stems from the weight of Utku’s absence and Aralık’s demand to know “Then what happened?” to MJ, but the lines usually aim at explicit explanation (defining ‘Five Eyes’ or ‘Demagogue’) rather than layered subtext, so the consequences of these conversations lean more toward information transfer than psychological shift.

recommendations.action: Trim some dialogue exchanges to enhance clarity and impact.

evidence.snippet: I needed to feel his energy and was desperate to know if he was there waiting for me.
Although my mourning was deep and profound, I decided to continue living and not take my own life...
He understood well the pull and power of money and the lengths that people would go to get to it.

criteria.rationale: Themes of grief, queer kinship, systemic criminalization, and Desire’s untameable pull are tightly interwoven as the unnamed narrator moves from Utku’s death and the Istanbul highlands with Irmak, to MJ’s body-cavity smuggling and fear of the L3 Communications scanner, to teaching Aralık about Five Eyes and the Demagogue. The pressure arises from the collision between personal love—naming Aralık after a Turkish month learned with Utku—and the impersonal reach of United Empire drug statutes and surveillance, which MJ must navigate. Consequences of these themes land in the father’s choice to raise Aralık in the shadow of Utku and to expose him to MJ’s ambiguity, though MJ’s actual meeting with Desire is left for another time, deferring one of the key thematic nodes.

recommendations.action: Consider weaving in additional thematic layers related to societal issues to deepen the narrative’s impact.

evidence.snippet: The valley floor had been tamed into obedience decades prior and was now a bed of tranquility.
They really became close though after high school when they had both moved to Istanbul.
The valley floor had been tamed into obedience decades prior and was now a bed of tranquility...

criteria.rationale: The blend of real-world geographies—Istanbul’s Asian-side highlands, Toronto, and the Coachella Valley—with a thinly veiled geopolitical map of the United Empire, Land Down-under, True North, Brexit, and Kiwi Land, and the ominous Five Eyes network creates a convincing environment for MJ’s smuggling and for the unnamed narrator’s lessons to Aralık. The L3 Communications millimeter wave machine, the network of cameras, and the Dark Overlords under the Demagogue all reinforce a dense system of control surrounding Desire’s festival. Pressure builds as MJ moves bodily through this apparatus and as Aralık tries to grasp that Five Eyes is “a signals intelligence alliance,” but at times the explanation of these systems overruns the dramatized experience of MJ and the young fawn inside them.

recommendations.symptom: Sensory grounding is absent from the passage, preventing the reader from anchoring in the physical setting.
recommendations.mechanism: Missing sensory detail forces the reader to imagine the setting without authorial guidance, increasing cognitive load.

Pressure around MJ’s body-cavity package and the young fawn builds well up to his refusal of the millimeter wave scan and his walk to the airplane washroom, but the decision to end the embedded story exactly there slows the sense of forward movement even as it preserves thematic ambiguity.

evidence.snippet: The congregation was far from civilization – allowing them to partake - without judgment - in rituals and ceremonies...
He was lithe, wearing beige cotton pants and a loose fitting, long-sleeved, white collar-less shirt.
He had a fascination with the macabre and criminal elements.
Aralık is of course my son.
Aralık means December in Turkish.

criteria.rationale: Line-level craft is generally strong: the unnamed narrator’s description of sun-parched hills in the Istanbul highlands, the valley below, and the ‘anthropomorphic deity of Desire’ showcases careful diction and rhythm, while the technical explanations of L3 Communications scanners and Five Eyes are syntactically clear. Utku’s memory and Irmak’s proposal are articulated in controlled, emotionally precise sentences, and MJ’s fears about his body-cavity package and United Empire law are rendered with intelligible detail. However, some long, heavily subordinated sentences in the airport exposition and the Five Eyes explanation can feel dense on first read, slightly obscuring MJ and the young fawn’s immediate sensory experiences and softening the impact of otherwise effective pacing choices.

evidence.snippet: I needed to feel his energy and was desperate to know if he was there waiting for me.
Although my mourning was deep and profound, I decided to continue living and not take my own life...
I am a happy single parent.

criteria.rationale: The tonal register coherently blends melancholy, moral seriousness, and flashes of wry humor across both the frame and the embedded story: the unnamed narrator’s grief for Utku and reverence for the sacred Istanbul highlands coexist with playful banter as Aralık bargains for more time by defining ‘right-wing,’ and with a lightly sardonic attitude toward the Demagogue, Dark Overlords, and Five Eyes. MJ’s tense progress past the L3 Communications machine and among the Empire’s security guards is narrated in a sober, controlled tone that respects the risk without sensationalizing it. This creates pressure as the text must remain suitable for a bedtime story while discussing body-cavity smuggling and Desire’s hedonistic desert festival; the choice to stop MJ’s story before any explicit sex, violence, or catastrophe helps preserve tonal balance, though it also keeps some potential intensity off-page.

recommendations.action: Rewrite one tonal outlier sentence to match the established register of the surrounding passage; The prose rhythm around the passage near “I needed to feel his…” is close to landing; a shorter concrete sentence after the reflective line because the tone stays largely in a mid-range of seriousness and controlled wit, some of.
recommendations.symptom: The passage tonal register shifts mid-passage without a clear trigger, disrupting emotional continuity
recommendations.mechanism: the tonal register shifts mid-passage without a clear trigger, disrupting emotional continuity
recommendations.specific_fix: rewrite one tonal outlier sentence to match the established register of the surrounding passage

evidence.snippet: That’s the end of the story. You have to come to your conclusions about the ending.
Did MJ leave the drugs in his cavity? Was he eventually caught?

criteria.rationale: Within this excerpt, the frame provides partial closure by situating the unnamed narrator as a ‘happy single parent’ in Toronto, having fulfilled Irmak’s wish from the Istanbul highlands and honored Utku through naming Aralık and revisiting their sacred meeting place. MJ’s embedded arc, however, remains broadly unresolved: he clears the United Empire’s airport, sits near the young fawn on the plane, and stands up to go to the washroom with contraband still inside him when the narrator abruptly ends the story and tells Aralık that he must draw his own conclusions. The pressure built around MJ’s body-cavity package, his fear of L3 Communications technology, his destination at Desire’s festival, and the ethical questions Aralık raises therefore sees its consequences deferred rather than landing concretely on the page.

recommendations.action: Provide clearer resolutions or hints at future developments to satisfy reader curiosity.

evidence.snippet: He understood well the pull and power of money and the lengths that people would go to get to it.
The congregation was far from civilization – allowing them to partake - without judgment - in rituals...

criteria.rationale: From a market perspective, the hybrid of queer grief memoir (Utku and Irmak in Istanbul, the unnamed narrator’s single parenthood in Toronto), literary crime (MJ’s body-cavity smuggling through the United Empire’s L3 Communications security system), and political-philosophical reflection (Five Eyes, Dark Overlords, the Demagogue) positions the work for readers of literary fiction interested in surveillance and Desire. The emotional bond between the narrator and Aralık and the intellectual rigor of the Five Eyes explanations are likely to appeal to a niche but committed audience. However, uneven pacing, the deliberate lack of closure around MJ’s fate and Desire’s festival, and the density of expository passages may make it a harder sell to broader markets that expect clearer narrative payoffs even in literary-leaning crime stories.

top_3_risks: Narrative momentum around MJ’s smuggling attempt and his foreshadowed meeting with Desire builds pressure but then defers or withholds concrete consequences, which may leave readers—and Aralık—unsatisfied.

Expository passages about United Empire drug statutes, Five Eyes, and L3 Communications sometimes displace scene-driven beats with MJ, the young fawn, or the Empire’s security guards, softening tension even for readers tolerant of reflection.

Dialogue in key teaching moments between the unnamed narrator and Aralık, and in Irmak’s proposal to have a child, leans toward didactic explanation rather than subtext-rich exchange, occasionally flattening character dynamics.
top_3_strengths: The frame-story concept that pairs a queer, grieving parent with a morally ambiguous smuggler, MJ, creates a rich platform to interrogate criminality, Desire, and systemic surveillance through Aralık’s questions.

The narrative voice—an introspective, gently didactic first person from the unnamed narrator—sustains intimacy while moving fluidly between Toronto, Istanbul’s highlands, and the Coachella Valley, anchoring Utku and Irmak in emotionally credible ways.

Thematic integration is strong, as the text braids Utku’s death, Irmak’s proposal, MJ’s body-cavity smuggling, Desire’s festival, and Five Eyes into a coherent argument about how love, grief, and systems intersect.
overview.one_sentence_pitch: A grieving Toronto father uses a suspenseful tale about MJ, a would-be drug smuggler bound for a desert festival of Desire, to teach his curious son Aralık that criminality is tangled with love, loss, and the oppressive systems watching them all.
overview.one_paragraph_pitch: In present-day Toronto, a retired Pratt & Whitney Canada employee and happy single parent reads a homemade bedtime story to his fourteen-year-old son, Aralık, hoping to answer the boy’s questions about whether MJ is a bad person. As the unnamed narrator recalls his lost lover Utku, his agreement with pansexual friend Irmak to conceive a child, and their sacred meeting place in the Istanbul highlands, the embedded tale follows MJ from a cold homeland through a gauntlet of cameras, metal detectors, and an L3 Communications millimeter wave machine toward a hedonistic Coachella Valley festival dev
overview.one_paragraph_summary: This excerpt lands at 65/100 largely because Narrative Drive & Momentum, Pacing & Structural Balance, and Narrative Closure lag behind stronger work in Concept & Core Premise, Thematic Integration, and Point of View & Voice Control. The most robust elements to preserve are the intimate, didactic voice between the unnamed narrator and Aralık and the layered thematic weave of queer grief, systemic criminalization, and Desire’s pull. The principal blocker to market readiness is the structural pattern in which MJ’s smuggling arc and the teased meeting with Desire accrue pressure but then dissipate without concrete on-page consequence, weakening narrative propulsion relative to the excerpt’s ambitions.

enrichment.premise: A bereaved Toronto father who once loved Utku and conceived a child with their pansexual friend Irmak now reads his teenage son a story about MJ, a smuggler bound for a desert festival of Desire, to explore how love, law, and surveillance shape what we call crime. As MJ hides pills in his body to slip past the United Empire’s L3 Communications scanners and Five Eyes, the boy presses for answers his father is not yet ready to give.
enrichment.target_audience: Readers of literary fiction interested in queer family-making, systemic critiques of surveillance and criminalization, and morally ambiguous crime narratives, similar to audiences for writers who blend memoir-like intimacy with political and philosophical inquiry.
